That's because we've hit the context window limit for this specific conversation. This was a really big problem in the early days of LLMs. These days it's not as big of a problem because modern models have massive context windows. So GBT 5.5 has a context window approximately equal to three textbooks. Claude Opus 4.8 and Gemini 2.5 Pro have about 7 and 12 textbooks worth of information. And then Cursor Composer 2 can process about 1.5 textbooks worth of text. So this is a massive amount of information that these models can process. However, when you use these models, they don't quote the context windows in textbooks or words. They quote them in a different unit which is shown here, which are actually so-called tokens. And a token is just a unit of text that an LLM can understand. Considering that paragraph of text we saw on the previous slide, this is what we might see. We might see our input here and then the output generated by the LLM. But from the LLM's perspective, it's not this paragraph of text, but rather the text gets chunked up into these smaller pieces called tokens. So Q becomes a token, three becomes a token, space numbers becomes a token, colon becomes a token, and so on and so forth. And then each of these units of text get mapped to a integer. So this process is called tokenization and the reason we have to do this is because an LLM runs on a computer and computers don't understand English. Computers understand numbers. So we need to make this translation from text into numbers so that the LLM can do these mathematical operations on the text in order to produce a prediction. So this is why when you're using tools like Claude or you're interacting with APIs, costs and usage limits are going to be quoted in tokens, not words or sentences or paragraphs or anything like that. What makes this possible is the so-called agent harness. And so these are the instructions, tools, and software built around an LLM. For example, if we have claude the LLM, the harness will be everything like giving it the ability to search the web, giving it the ability to write and run code, giving it access to local file system so it can read and write files, and then also giving it guard rails. So things like requiring the model to get permission before executing a specific tool call, preventing the agent from reading specific folders or files, and then just instructions that help Claude stay on track and stay helpful. While the underlying model itself is a key part of modern AI agents, what's just as important is the harness. The second tip is to start small. So in other words, get the use case working once before trying to scale it up. There's any number of things that can go wrong. And so what will probably end up happening is you're going to spend hours fixing the automation before it actually ends up working.
